g.data-narration: Welcome to Kruskal's Algorithm - a greedy approach to find the Minimum Spanning Tree of a weighted graph.
g.text: Finding the Minimum Spanning Tree using Greedy Approach

g.data-narration: A Minimum Spanning Tree connects all vertices with minimum total weight, using exactly V-1 edges without cycles.
g.text: A spanning tree contains ALL vertices with exactly (V-1) edges and no cycles.
MST = Spanning tree with MINIMUM total edge weight.

g.data-narration: Kruskal's Algorithm sorts edges by weight and adds them one by one, skipping those that would form cycles.
g.text: A greedy algorithm that builds MST by selecting edges in increasing order of weight.
Uses Union-Find data structure for efficient cycle detection.

g.data-narration: Union-Find maintains disjoint sets with Find (returns root) and Union (merges sets) operations for cycle detection.
g.text: Efficient data structure for cycle detection in Kruskal's Algorithm.

g.data-narration: Before adding an edge, we check if both vertices are in the same set using Find - if yes, adding creates a cycle.
g.text: For each edge (u, v) in sorted order:
• If Find(u) ≠ Find(v): Add edge to MST, then Union(u, v)
• If Find(u) = Find(v): Skip edge (would create cycle)

g.data-narration: The algorithm has four main steps: sort edges, initialize Union-Find, process edges, and stop when V-1 edges are added.
g.text: Initialize Union-Find: each vertex is its own parent
For each edge: Check Find(u) vs Find(v), Union if different, else skip
Stop when MST has exactly (V-1) edges or all edges processed

g.data-narration: Let's trace Kruskal's on a graph with vertices A, B, C, D and edges with various weights.
g.text: Example: Find MST for this Graph

g.data-narration: Processing AB(10): different sets, add to MST and union. Then BC(15): still different sets, add and union.
Processing CD(20): Find(C)≠Find(D), add to MST. Now we have V-1 edges, MST is complete!
Time complexity is O(E log E) primarily from sorting edges, with O(E α(V)) for nearly constant Union-Find operations.
Kruskal's works edge-by-edge, ideal for sparse graphs. Prim's grows from a vertex, better for dense graphs.
g.text: Best for: Sparse graphs, E ≈ V
Best for: Dense graphs, E ≈ V²

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: Greedy approach, Union-Find for cycles, O(E log E) complexity, builds MST edge by edge.
g.text: • Greedy algorithm: locally optimal choices → global optimum
• Uses Union-Find for efficient cycle detection
• Time: O(E log E), Space: O(V + E)
CSE - Analysis and Design of Algorithms
